variables.name: Anchor / rule-writer for the order

actor_readings.reading: Has vacated the liberal-anchor role and is now the primary source of managed-trade fragmentation, high confidence (#7,#A40). Retains coercive market-access leverage but no intent to re-anchor liberalism.

Only actor offering a rule-writing function post-hegemonically, via regulatory reach (#46,#22), but cannot supply security/liquidity public goods (#1). Medium confidence it can partially fill the vacuum, not fully.
Building parallel rule-architecture (BRI standards, RCEP, mBridge) while using WTO selectively; a co-anchor candidate only under negotiated bipolarity (#10,#49). Ambiguous.

variables.current_reading: Sitting between 'US-led managed-mercantilist' and 'no liberal anchor', high confidence. The order's architect has affirmatively abandoned comparative-advantage doctrine (IRA/CHIPS/2025 tariffs, #7) so the normative foundation, not just compliance, is gone. The EU projects binding rules via market access (GDPR/CBAM/AI Act, #46,#22) but lacks strategic/security coherence to be a liberal hegemon (#1). No replacement anchor exists.
variables.trajectory: Directional away from liberal anchoring, slow-moving and NOT easily reversible — hegemonic willingness, once withdrawn, historically does not return without a strategic pivot by the anchor itself (UK's EEC pivot took decades, #A30; Pax Britannica hollowed out rather than snapped, #A3). A US re-embedding would require the 1934 RTAA-style deliberate intent that is currently absent (#A15,#A26). Clock: 10-20 years, election-cycle contingent.
variables.base_rate: In prior tech/trade orders the anchor's exit hollowed the order from within over ~decades (Pax Britannica 1890s-1914 #A3; ITO death but GATT survived 47yrs #A20). Base rate says institutional shell persists while the bargain dissolves. Breaks: the Brussels Effect (#46) is genuinely novel — rule-writing without military/currency hegemony has no clean historical precedent.

variables.name: US–China power-transition trajectory

actor_readings.reading: Treats the arrangement as net-negative and disables institutions rather than reforming (#A27); prefers managed/bilateral first (#A40). Confident.
Rising but decelerating (#54); export-model pressure generates the surpluses that trigger countermeasures (#48). Incentive to negotiate rises as growth slows. Medium confidence.

variables.current_reading: Between 'acute parity conflict' and early 'peak-China deceleration', medium confidence. Trade war is epiphenomenal to the transition (#3): it is about who writes the next rules. Demographic/property/productivity headwinds (#54) and a contested export-to-consumption pivot (#48) may be truncating the parity window, which raises Beijing's opportunity cost of protracted conflict and makes a framework deal more attractive.
variables.trajectory: Direction: toward either codified bipolarity or long bifurcation; conflict is structurally overdetermined until the transition resolves (#3). Clock is the slowest of all variables (decade+). Peak-China dynamics (#54) could shorten the disruptive interim. The closest precedent (US-Japan #A4,#A47) converged because Japan was a subordinate ally — China is not, so convergence is far less catch-up-able.
variables.base_rate: Power-transition base rate: rivalry over rules peaks at parity approach and does not settle until transition completes (#3, Organski/Copeland). US-Japan 1986-95 managed trade converged (#A4,#A47); Hamilton/List US-UK case renegotiated over decades + a world war (#A6). Breaks: nuclear deterrence removes the WWII-style 'reset' that cleared prior transitions (#A1 different), forcing a longer managed interim.

variables.name: Security-driven fragmentation logic (weaponization of interdependence)

actor_readings.reading: Primary wielder on the demand/buyer side (chip controls, outbound investment, #62,#82) — a postwar first. High confidence this is a durable structural policy, not tactical.
Primary wielder on the supplier side (gallium/germanium/graphite/rare-earth licensing, #69) — mirror-image weaponization. High confidence.
Reluctant fragmenter — CBAM (#22) and screening add fracture lines but MNC/hyperscaler exposure (#8,#64) pulls against hard decoupling. Medium confidence, hedging.

variables.current_reading: At 'strategic-sector carve-outs' escalating toward 'pervasive tech-stack bifurcation', high confidence. Export controls (#18,#62), dollar/reserve weaponization (#19), inbound + outbound investment screening (#81,#82), and defense-industrial-base logic (#25) constitute a security rationale absent from the original liberal design (#6) and structurally WTO-immune (self-judging Art XXI, #18,#79). This layer is largely one-directional/ratchet.
variables.trajectory: Direction: escalating and hard to reverse — governments now rationally invest in redundancy/autarky as insurance (#6); controls are hardware-embedded (#62) and screening scope only ratchets (#87). Clock: fast to impose (policy stroke), slow-to-impossible to unwind. Network-effect/tech bifurcation (#63) is the least catch-up-able; goods rerouting the most.
variables.base_rate: COCOM ran a genuine tech bifurcation for 45yrs and only liberalized when the adversary collapsed (#A32); SWIFT/dollar weaponization accelerated parallel-system building (#A17). Base rate: security carve-outs institutionalize for decades (MFA 30yrs #A9; agriculture 39yrs #A42). Breaks: China is INSIDE the system (WTO, supply chains) unlike the USSR outside COCOM — enforcement leaks and coalition unity is the binding constraint.

variables.name: Supply-chain integration floor (stickiness, agglomeration, rerouting)
variables.current_reading: Between 'China+1 partial' and 'rerouting-only', medium-high confidence. Trillions in sunk MNC costs and agglomeration lock-in (#8,#23) plus friend-shoring announcements outrunning actual capex (#26) mean the 2018-2025 record is diversification + relabeling, not exit. Mexico became #1 US partner via Chinese-input reprocessing (#35,#38); goods deficit hit records despite tariffs (#A48). This is a real brake on V3.
variables.trajectory: Direction: slow physical reorganization but fast statistical rerouting. Firms route around managed trade via FDI/third countries every prior episode (#A43-#A48). Clock: goods supply chains rewire in 5-10yrs given capital; the FDI escape valve is being deliberately closed for Chinese firms via screening (#A48,#81) — the one genuinely novel, less catch-up-able element.
variables.base_rate: Every managed-trade episode produced GVC workaround not suppression: Japanese auto transplants (#A43), EC screwdriver plants (#A44), MFA quota-jumping created new industries (#A46), maquiladora (#A45), Plaza→Factory Asia (#A47), Section 301→Vietnam/Mexico (#A48). Base rate: trade reroutes, volumes persist. Breaks: China's scale (~19% of manufacturing) makes genuine reshoring an order of magnitude costlier, and investment screening has no precedent for staying closed at this scale.

variables.name: Institutional adaptation capacity (WTO / dispute settlement / plurilaterals)
variables.current_reading: At 'rump-but-functional' trending toward 'zombie', medium confidence. Appellate Body dead since Dec 2019 (#12); but MPIA now ~53 members (#9), first new multilateral deal in 25yrs (fisheries #33), JSIs at 80-90 members (#33), CPTPP/RCEP filling the US vacuum (#9,#A24). Simultaneously e-commerce moratorium nearly lapsed (#61,#68) and Art XXI is self-judging (#79). Degradation, not destruction (#4).
variables.trajectory: Direction: enforcement backbone durably broken, trade-facilitation functions survive. Clock: AB paralysis is more durable than prior blockages because the US now prefers managed trade (#A40 vs GATT-era US wanting liberalization). Great-power disputes become advisory, middle-power disputes stay binding (two-tier, #A55,#A57,#A58). Not catch-up-able without a US strategic pivot (V1).
variables.base_rate: GATT survived 47yrs institutionally fragile (#A20); consensus-blocking + Section 301 unilateralism is a documented US playbook (#A40); ICJ/UNCLOS/League show major-power defection hollows the mechanism permanently, never restored to prior authority (#A56,#A55,#A59). Doha collapse migrated action to PTAs (#A12). Base rate: zombie/rump, not clean death. Breaks: MPIA is a more institutionalized workaround than GATT-era side-deals — possibly more resilient.

variables.name: Domestic political economy & compensation bargain
state_space: durable anti-trade majority (demand-side, decades to shift)

actor_readings.reading: Cross-class protectionist coalition entrenched across both parties (#2,#A40); managed-trade rents captured by capital undermine the labor coalition (#57). Demand-side turn looks durable. Medium-high confidence.
Rust-belt/populist pressure real (Le Pen) but single-market membership and CBAM-style measures channel it into managed rather than exit outcomes. Medium confidence.

variables.current_reading: Closer to 'durable demand-side turn', medium confidence, but genuinely ambiguous (#11). Distributional losers organized cross-class coalitions (#2); the embedded-liberalism compensation bargain collapsed (deindustrialized workers got no adjustment, #15). Critically, managed-trade subsidies are flowing to capital not labor (#57,#39), so even the managed-trade coalition may dissolve before reconsolidation — no viable new compensation mechanism yet exists (#53).
variables.trajectory: Direction: if demand-side (public turn), no institutional reform restores the order — requires decades of realignment; if supply-side, targeted reform works (#11). Clock: political realignment is the slowest social variable. Every liberalization wave generated organized losers who drove durable backlash (#A22,#A28) — sticky, low catch-up-ability.
variables.base_rate: EU Eastern enlargement backlash (#A22) and Latin American ISI-reversal backlash (#A28) show distributional losers destabilize integration for a generation even with safeguards; the WTO integrated China with NO compensation mechanism (#A22,#A27). Base rate: demand-side turns are durable. Breaks: Brexit showed demonstrated costs can DETER imitation and re-stabilize a bloc (#A16), cutting the other way.

variables.name: Monetary settlement layer (dollar reserve-currency status)

actor_readings.reading: Wielder of dollar weaponization; each use accelerates long-run erosion of its own leverage (#19,#A17). Structurally dominant near-term. Confident.
Core mBridge participants (UAE, Saudi, #65) hedging into non-dollar settlement while retaining dollar ties — connector-style ambiguity. Medium confidence.
Building RMB/CBDC alternatives but constrained by capital controls and inconvertibility (#A17). Slow progress.

variables.current_reading: At 'eroding-but-dominant', medium confidence. Dollar share of reserves fell ~72% (2001) to ~58% (2024) (#A17); Russia's $300bn freeze (2022) proved dollar assets confiscable, incentivizing diversification (#19,#24). mBridge is operational (real-value pilots 2024), China-anchored, geographically tracking Gulf/SE-Asia/connector economies (#65). But network effects and yuan inconvertibility keep the dollar structurally dominant (#A17).
variables.trajectory: Direction: slow erosion, faster at the margins where sanctioned/hedging states build parallels. Clock: dollar network effects are far harder to dislodge than tariffs (#A17); de-dollarization is a multi-decade process. mBridge (#65) makes weaponization durably less coercive over ~5-10yrs. Degrades independently of tariff policy (#24).
variables.base_rate: Bretton Woods monetary collapse (1971) yet dollar stayed dominant via network effects and G7 coordination — a rule broke but the currency persisted (#A2). Base rate: reserve status is extremely sticky. Breaks: no prior era had an operational state-backed CBDC bridge (#65) — the parallel plumbing is genuinely new.

variables.name: Critical-input chokepoints (chips, mineral processing)

actor_readings.reading: Controls the chip chokepoint but is the DEPENDENT party on mineral processing (near-zero domestic lithium-hydroxide capacity, #72). This is its binding material constraint. High confidence.
Controls the processing chokepoint but is the DEPENDENT party on leading-edge logic and EUV (#40,#62). Mirror-image bind. High confidence.
Dependent on BOTH chokepoints, controls neither at scale (CRMA nascent); most exposed to the standoff. Medium-high confidence.

variables.current_reading: At 'mutual-chokepoint standoff', high confidence. US-allied bloc controls sub-5nm logic (~90% on Taiwan, #40) and EUV/EAR licensing (#62); China controls ~80% rare-earth processing, ~70% cobalt refining, ~60%+ battery precursor (#70) and mirrors chip controls from the supplier side (#69). Each holds a gun to the other's strategic base; neither can decouple without severe self-harm this decade.
variables.trajectory: Direction: entrenching on both sides. Chips: US lead is fast-eroding but 5-10yr replacement horizon for any node (#40). Minerals: mining diversifiable but PROCESSING requires 10-15yr builds + toxic-waste permitting (#72,#73) — geology is a hard ceiling (#73). Processing moat is the LEAST catch-up-able chokepoint; a kinetic Taiwan event (#40) is the one irreversible fast-shock.
variables.base_rate: OPEC oil weapon was blunted in ~12yrs by substitution but at huge dislocation (#A8); China rare-earth WTO loss (2014) didn't restore supply security — Western chains still China-processing-dependent a decade later (#A37). IEA/MSP counter-cartel is a 10-15yr project (#A39). Base rate: chokepoints hold for 1-2 decades then get bypassed. Breaks: processing concentration + geology has no goods-trade analogue (#73) — potentially more durable.

variables.name: Swing-state / connector alignment (India, Global South, connectors)

actor_readings.reading: The pivotal swing: rejected RCEP, ambivalent on CPTPP, high tariffs, explicit strategic autonomy (#30). If it declines to join, no US-led bloc reaches manufacturing critical mass. Medium-high confidence it stays non-aligned near-term.
Connector/hedging strategy — mBridge participant + US security ties (#65,#36). Extracting rents from ambiguity. Medium confidence.
Holds upstream intermediate-input leverage over connectors (#42) — can threaten the connector equilibrium from the supply side. Under-exercised so far.

variables.current_reading: At 'connector equilibrium' with 'durable non-alignment', medium confidence. India, Brazil, Vietnam, Indonesia, Gulf trade actively with both sides and hedging is INCREASING not decreasing (#20). Connectors pursue calculated ambiguity as sovereign rent-strategy (#36). India is the decisive swing (scale to deny any US bloc critical mass, #30). But connector rents are geographically concentrated (#41) and threatened by rules-of-origin enforcement (#37) and Chinese input leverage (#42).
variables.trajectory: Direction: neutrality currently rational and increasing, but window may be closing as blocs consolidate and rents get squeezed (#43). Clock: connector rents are transitional — depend on a bloc-building-incompleteness window that closes as IRA/CHIPS/EU capacity comes online (#43). Rules-of-origin enforcement (#37) is the fast institutional variable that could close arbitrage.
variables.base_rate: Interwar blocs (#A5) and Zollverein (#A10) forced middle powers to choose as blocs deepened; but today's swing states have more agency (#A5 different, #20). Base rate: consolidating hegemonic blocs narrow non-alignment over time. Breaks: modern connectors have deliberate rent-strategies and China+1 gives them leverage prior middle powers lacked (#36).

variables.name: Managed-trade fiscal & state-capacity sustainability

actor_readings.reading: Fiscal ceiling (#32) + eroded state capacity (#34) + capital-capture of subsidies (#57) make its managed-trade turn self-limiting or rent-capturing rather than strategically welfare-improving. Medium confidence.

Retains developmental-state capacity to execute industrial policy (#34) but faces SOE/provincial resistance to the consumption pivot (#48). Better-positioned to sustain managed trade than Western democracies.

variables.current_reading: Between 'self-limiting transitional' and 'rent-capture', medium confidence. Strategic-trade theory gives managed trade a welfare rationale (#16), but execution needs developmental-state capacity the US/UK/EU atrophied (#34); subsidies flow to incumbents/capital (#39,#57,#58), risking Stigler-capture rather than strategic equilibrium. Fiscal ceiling (US debt >120% GDP, #32) and structural oligopoly lock-in (#59) are self-limiting mechanisms.
variables.trajectory: Direction: managed trade entrenches politically (incumbents write rent rules, #39,#58) even as fiscal capacity erodes. Clock: fab/subsidy structures lock in over 5-10yrs, after which dismantling domestic oligopoly rents is far harder than cutting tariffs (#59). Fiscal consolidation pressure builds over election cycles (#32). Two competing clocks — political entrenchment vs fiscal exhaustion.
variables.base_rate: Agriculture (#A42, 39yrs), MFA (#A9, 30yrs), Imperial Preference (#A30, 40yrs) show sectoral managed trade institutionalizes for decades once incumbents capture it. Latin American ISI ran 3-4 decades before an external debt crisis forced reversal (#A28). Base rate: managed trade is sticky for decades. Breaks: reserve-currency issuers face no IMF-conditionality forcing mechanism (#A28 different) — stickier still, but fiscal math (#32) is a novel self-limit.

edges.note: Power-transition rivalry is the upstream driver; it is what converts trade ties into coercive/security instruments — decoupling follows the transition, not efficiency (#3,#6).
Security logic guts dispute settlement: self-judging Art XXI (#18,#79) and hardware-embedded controls (#62) are structurally WTO-unchallengeable, disabling the enforcement backbone.
Supply-chain cost floor caps how far security-driven decoupling can physically proceed — agglomeration + sunk costs make full separation prohibitively expensive (#8,#23,#A48).
Institutional restoration requires an anchor with intent to lead; the US exit (#7,#A40) blocks AB restoration — no anchor, no rebuild (#A20,#A26).
Plurilateral clubs (CPTPP/RCEP/MPIA/JSIs) and EU regulatory reach partially substitute for the absent hegemon, supplying rules without a single anchor (#9,#33,#46,#A24).
Whether the US can resume any liberal-anchor role is gated by its domestic coalition; a durable demand-side turn forecloses re-anchoring for a generation (#2,#11,#A22).
Managed trade's durability depends on a sustaining coalition; subsidies captured by capital not labor dissolve that coalition before reconsolidation (#57,#39).
The Taiwan/TSMC leading-edge chokepoint (US-allied) is the acute contingency that could force rapid hard bloc formation as survival response (#40).
China's ~80% processing moat (#70) sits beneath supply-chain separation — a floor no connector rerouting can bypass (#70), constraining how much V4 diversification is real.
Mutual chokepoints (US chips vs China minerals, #62 vs #69) create a MAD-like standoff — each actor's weapon deters the other's full use, mutually capping decoupling this decade.
Dollar weaponization (#19) is both an instance of security logic and its accelerant — each use drives parallel-plumbing build (mBridge #65), degrading the settlement layer (#24).
Swing-state alignment determines whether the transition completes as clean bifurcation or stays plural — India's scale can deny any US bloc critical mass (#30,#A5,#A10).
China's upstream intermediate-input dominance over connectors (Vietnamese electronics, Mexican parts, #42) lets it threaten the connector equilibrium from the supply side.
Rules-of-origin enforcement capacity (an institutional variable, #37) determines whether connector arbitrage persists or closes — cosmetic vs structural fragmentation.
Fiscal ceiling + oligopoly lock-in (#32,#59) set whether the managed/security regime is a durable order or a self-limiting transitional episode.
Near-parity means no single power can anchor a successor monetary order alone (#A2 different), reinforcing settlement-layer fragmentation as the transition proceeds.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will a WTO dispute involving the US or China be resolved with a binding appellate ruling (AB restored or via MPIA covering that party) before 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if, by 2027-12-31, either the WTO Appellate Body has functioning members hearing a US- or China-party appeal, or an MPIA/equivalent binding award is issued in a dispute where the US or China is respondent. Source: WTO dispute-settlement docket.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will the US dollar share of allocated global FX reserves fall below 55% by Q4 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if IMF COFER data for Q4 2027 reports USD share of allocated reserves below 55.0%. Source: IMF COFER.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will the US maintain or expand (no net rollback of) advanced-semiconductor export controls on China through 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if BIS Entity List / EAR advanced-computing chip controls in force are equal to or broader than the Oct-2023 baseline as of 2027-12-31, with no wholesale rescission. Source: US BIS Federal Register rules.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will China keep export-licensing controls on gallium, germanium, or rare-earth processing technology in force through 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if China's MOFCOM maintains licensing/export controls on at least one of gallium, germanium, graphite, antimony, or rare-earth separation technology as of 2027-12-31. Source: MOFCOM announcements.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will India formally accede to CPTPP or a US-led plurilateral goods-trade bloc by 31 Dec 2028?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if India signs an accession instrument to CPTPP or a comparable US-anchored plurilateral FTA with binding market-access commitments by 2028-12-31. Source: official treaty texts / government announcements.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will combined US goods imports from Mexico + Vietnam exceed direct US goods imports from China through calendar 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if US Census full-year 2027 goods-import data show Mexico + Vietnam combined exceeding China. Source: US Census Bureau trade data.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will the US and China sign a formal bilateral framework agreement codifying tariff floors and/or tech-transfer rules by 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if a signed US-China intergovernmental framework text on tariffs/technology/investment is published by 2027-12-31 (beyond a phase-one-style purchase deal). Source: USTR / official joint statements.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will the WTO customs-duty moratorium on electronic transmissions be made permanent (not merely renewed) by the next Ministerial Conference?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if a WTO Ministerial decision makes the e-commerce moratorium permanent by the conclusion of the next MC. Source: WTO Ministerial outcome documents.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will the US materially roll back IRA/CHIPS subsidy commitments (net reduction in authorized/obligated funds) by 31 Dec 2028?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if enacted legislation or executive action reduces total authorized IRA clean-energy + CHIPS manufacturing incentives below the FY2025 baseline by 2028-12-31. Source: CBO / Treasury / Commerce budget records.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will any G7 government take office on a platform whose core plank is trade re-liberalization by 31 Dec 2028?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if, by 2028-12-31, a G7 national government is formed whose published manifesto centrally advocates reducing tariffs / restoring multilateral free trade as a headline commitment. Source: party manifestos and election results.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will the EU CBAM enter its full definitive (financial-liability) phase on schedule by 1 Jan 2027 without material weakening?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: Yes if CBAM certificate purchase obligations take effect for covered sectors from 2026-2027 without a delay or scope-gutting amendment. Source: EU Official Journal / Commission implementing acts.

binding_constraint_by_actor.reading: Minimum stave = critical-mineral PROCESSING dependence on China (V8, #70,#72) compounded by its own fiscal/state-capacity ceiling (V10, #32,#34,#59). The US controls the chip chokepoint but cannot clear its processing dependence for 10-15yrs (geology + permitting, #73), and its managed-trade order is fiscally and politically self-limiting. Restoration of any liberal order is separately gated by its domestic coalition (V6).

Minimum stave = leading-edge logic-chip access (V8, #40,#62), secondarily dollar settlement (V7) and the export-to-consumption transition (V2, #48). China holds the processing chokepoint but is bound by the ~5-10yr horizon to indigenize sub-5nm fabrication; the chip bind is its most acute, least catch-up-able constraint.

Minimum stave =strategic incoherence / lack of a security anchor (V1, #1) leaving it dependent on BOTH chokepoints it controls neither of (V8). Its only lever is regulatory rule-projection (Brussels Effect, #46,#22), which cannot substitute for hard-power public goods. It is the most exposed actor to the mutual-chokepoint standoff.

binding_constraint_by_actor.actor: Connectors / Global South (India, Vietnam, Mexico, Gulf)
binding_constraint_by_actor.reading: Minimum stave = rules-of-origin enforcement (V5→V9, #37) plus Chinese intermediate-input dependence (V9, #42) and narrow absorptive capacity (#41). Their connector-rent equilibrium is transitional (#43) — it clears (closes) when either enforcement tightens or bloc-building capacity comes online, whichever is faster. India uniquely has the scale to keep the whole system plural (#30).

summary: The graph does not resolve to "broken" vs "managed" — it resolves to a downgraded, plural equilibrium whose depth is set by which constraint clears first. The structural driver is the US–China power transition (V2), which spawns a security-fragmentation logic (V3) that the GATT/WTO rulebook cannot govern and that is largely one-way (weaponization, export controls, investment screening — #6,#18,#62,#81,#A32). Two brakes sit against full bifurcation: a supply-chain cost floor (V4 — agglomeration, rerouting, MNC lobbying, #8,#23,#35,#A43-#A48) and a pair of MUTUAL chokepoints (V8) — the US-allied bloc controls leading-edge logic (#40,#62) while China controls mineral processing (#69,#70), a MAD-like standoff neither can escape this decade. The order's restoration is gated on an absent anchor (V1): the US abandoned the rule-writer role (#7,#A3), the EU can only project regulatory (not liberal-hegemonic) rules (#46), so institutions degrade into plurilateral/rump form (V5 — MPIA, JSIs, RCEP/CPTPP; #9,#33,#A20,#A24) rather than dying. Whose constraint clears on what clock: the US is bound by mineral-processing dependence and its own fiscal/state-capacity ceiling (V8,V10 — 10-15yr clock #72,#32,#59); China by chip access and dollar settlement (V8,V7 — 5-10yr clock #40,#65); the EU by strategic incoherence; connectors/Global South by rules-of-origin enforcement and Chinese input dependence (V9 — #37,#42). Honest read: not permanently broken, not restorable to liberal multilateralism — durably MANAGED and plural, with the depth of fragmentation contested and clock-dependent.
